textPlain: Very interesting idea! I also replaced vector databases with "Table of Content" for retrieval: https://colab.research.google.com/github/VectifyAI/PageIndex...
I was working on memory consolidation and memory pruning 3 years ago (with GPT3 Davinci). Glad to see more people are working towards this.
I'm failing to grasp how it solves/replaces what vector db were created for in the first place (high-dimensional neighborhood searching, where the space to be searched grows by distance^dimension)

I think BM25 is the right path for most use cases. I'd reach for a single lucene index here, making the commit hash one of the document's indexed fields. This would make FTS across history nearly instant. I'd store it in an ignored folder right next to .git.For code search only, BM25 might be a bit overkill and not exactly what you want. FM indexes would be a simpler and faster way to implement pure substring search.Maybe having both kinds of search at the same time could work better than either in isolation. You could frame them as "semantic" and "exact" search from the perspective of the LLM tool calls. The prompt could then say things like "for searching the codebase use FunctionA, for searching requirements or issues, use FunctionB."

Using a vector db that’s created as a post commithook is probably a reasonable improvement. With context lengths getting bigger natural language is getting easier to work with but your leaving a bunch of gains on the floor without them.No shade on your project, this is an emerging space and we can all use novel approaches.Keep it up!

I'm no ai expert so consider my opinion having little value besides casual user.
But I really like this. It seems clever and easy for me to grasp some pros for this approach.I could envision a bunch of use cases about this workikg well. Ive personally encounter scenadios where sometimes the ai gets hung up on irrelevant outdated fact. But could still look up if specifically needed.I could see even an automated short summary of all history that is outdated being updated in the vector db from this too. So not all context is lost.Keep up the great work!
Just had a look at the repo -- I'm curious to know where this goes when you add vectors for semantic / hybrid search.The use of commit-hooks is also very clever (mentioned here in the replies)
Interesting idea, I like the fact that it doesn't use embeddings, and going into historical memory is an extra step that doesn't impact the speed of resolving the current information

I mean these are two completely different approaches for completely different purposes. So saying that git replaces vector database is like saying that that a filing cabinet replaces a search engines.
Seems really interesting! It would be great to see some evals to understand how well this works when stacked up against different approaches and use cases.
This is cool! Do you have any plans on releasing it as an MCP server? Would love to add this to my mcp gateway service https://mcp-boss.com/ (shameless plug)

Super simplistic example, but say i mention my Daughter, who is 9.Then mention she is 10,a few years later she is 12 but now i call her by her name.I have struggled to get any of the RAG approaches to handle this effectively. It is also 3 entries, but 2 of them are no longer useful, they are nothing but noise in the system.
It doesn't replace vector db, it's more for storing agentic memory, think of information which you would like agents to remember across conversations with users just like humans
Exactly. If you're not using embeddings, why would you need a vector db?> Why are we building complex vector storesBecause we want to use embeddings.
ooo nice post-commit creation of the db for the "now" state and links to the files for diff/historical? best of both worlds.
exactly!And you can _choose_ to explore the history, which in the most common case is not even needed.
Keen to do this as well! But couldn't find a good reliable eval for memory creation and retrieval, if you have one you could recommend i'd give it a go asap.
Would have to be productionized, the current repo is a PoC, but if there is enough interest we could def commit some resources to hardening this and adding things like MCP.
